title: Markup Calculator
shortDescription: Work out markup percent, selling price, and profit from your cost and target price.
metaDescription: Calculate markup percentage, selling price, and profit from cost or target price in one step.
intro: Enter your cost and either the markup rate or sale price to get the missing values instantly.
overview:
The markup calculator helps you turn a product cost into a selling price, or work backward from a sale price to the markup percent. It is useful when you need to set retail prices, compare supplier costs, or check whether a quoted price leaves enough room for profit.

You can use it for a single item or for repeated pricing tasks across a list of products. The result shows the markup amount, the final selling price, and the percentage so you can see exactly how the price was built.

If you already know your target margin strategy, this tool makes it easier to test different cost levels and pricing outcomes without doing the arithmetic by hand.
howItWorks: Add either the markup percentage or the selling price.
Review the calculated markup amount, final price, and profit figure.

useCases.description: Compare two purchase costs and see how each one changes the final selling price.

useCases.title: Bundle pricing
useCases.description: Calculate a price for a set or package when the total cost is known and you want a fixed markup.

examples.title: Price a handmade item
examples.description: Shows how to convert a craft cost into a retail price.

examples.title: Recover markup from sale price
examples.description: Useful when the final price is known and you want the implied markup.

examples.title: Check a wholesale offer
examples.description: Helps confirm a wholesale purchase still leaves the planned price room.

faq.question: What is the difference between markup and margin?
faq.answer: Markup is added on top of cost. Margin is measured against the selling price. The same numbers can produce different percentages, so do not swap them by mistake.

faq.question: Can I enter a selling price instead of a markup percent?
faq.answer: Yes, if the tool supports reverse calculation. That lets you see the implied markup from a final price and a known cost.

faq.question: Why do I get a decimal result?
faq.answer: Many costs do not divide evenly into whole numbers, so the tool may show cents or rounded decimals. Use the displayed rounding method when you set your price.

faq.question: What is the most common mistake when using markup?
faq.answer: Users often calculate markup on the wrong base. Always apply markup to cost unless your pricing method explicitly uses a different base.

faq.question: Does a higher markup always mean higher profit?
faq.answer: Not necessarily. Profit also depends on overhead, discounts, fees, and taxes. A large markup can still fail if other costs are high.
